checks.a_analysis: I carefully compared the text content of the provided JSON output against the source slide deck (represented by the 16 pages of OCR and images). Every heading, list item, and paragraph in the output corresponds to content found in the source. The titles used for sections and headers are pulled directly from the slide titles (e.g., 'Survey Background', 'Survey Solicitation', 'Q1: Which sector do you represent?'). The AI-generated alt text for images faithfully summarizes the visual data presented in the charts.

I verified the email addresses on the final slide (Page 16) against the output JSON. The email addresses 'DABrannan@cabarruscounty.us', 'anna.verrill@nc.gov', and 'tim.johnson@nc.gov' are correctly preserved in the 'Contacts' section of the output.

All 16 slides from the source document are accounted for in the output. Sections 0 through 9 correspond to the first 10 slides. Section 10 contains the content for slides 11 through 16 (Questions 8, 9, 10, the summary, and contact information), maintaining correct logical order.
The source document contains no tables; numerical data is presented via bar and pie charts, which are captured as images with descriptive alt text.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I conducted a spot check of lists across several slides. Page 11 contains 23 items across two columns; the output correctly lists all 23 in a single sequence. Page 7 (Q4) contains 8 items; the output has 8 items. Page 13 text regarding Differential Privacy is fully captured. No significant text omissions were found.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The output text accurately reflects the source. Formatting such as italics for the chair's name on Slide 1 and the superscript 'th' in the date on Slide 2 are correctly captured using semantic metadata.

checks.a_analysis: The document conversion was checked for any duplicated text blocks, paragraphs, or sections. The text content across all 16 slides appears only once in the JSON output, following the sequential structure of the presentation. No instances of unintentional repetition were found.

The heading hierarchy is broken starting from Page 11. Slides 1 through 10 are correctly placed in their own sections. However, Slides 11 through 16 are merged into a single section (Section 11). The title of Slide 11 is used as the section title, while the titles of Slides 12, 13, 14, 15, and 16 are rendered as H2 headings within that section. This incorrectly nests top-level slide titles (Q9, Q10, Summary, etc.) as subsections of Slide 11 (Q8), which is a major structural nesting error.
checks.b_evidence_source: Slide 12: Q9: If possible, provide evidence for how census is used for funding or services rates. (This is a slide title, equal in hierarchy to Slide 11's title)
checks.b_evidence_output: "type": "heading", "level": 2, "text": "Q9: If possible, provide evidence for how census is used for funding or services rates.", "id": "heading-3"

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains no tables. This check is not applicable.

The reading order of the converted document follows the logical progression of the original presentation slides. Even though the final slides are grouped into one section, they maintain the correct sequence from Page 11 through Page 16.

Lists throughout the document (Slides 2, 3, 7, 8, 9, 11, 14, 15) have been correctly identified and converted into JSON list structures. Nested lists (e.g., on Slide 2) are properly nested in the output. While the multi-column list on Slide 11 is flattened into a single list, the meaning and structure are preserved.
The contact email addresses on Slide 16 are correctly converted into functional hyperlinks with the proper 'mailto:' prefix. No broken or fabricated links were observed.

checks.a_analysis: The conversion from a presentation slide deck to a single-column HTML document is a standard responsive layout transition. The visual organization generally follows the logical flow of the source PDF, moving from the title slide through the survey questions to the summary. While the transition from side-by-side elements (like the cover logo) to a vertical stack is significant, it is a standard practice for web accessibility and does not fundamentally confuse the meaning of the content structure.
The source document contains no formal tables; it is composed of text, bulleted lists, and charts. Consequently, there are no table rendering defects in the HTML output.

The document contains several critical data visualizations (bar charts and a pie chart) that represent 50% of the survey's core findings. In the HTML output, every one of these charts (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q5, and Q7) is rendered as an empty solid green rectangle, completely losing all data points, labels, and axes. Additionally, the NCDIT organizational logo from the cover page is rendered as a massive, distorted black-and-blue block that dominates the page layout inappropriately. These are meaningful images that are effectively broken in the rendered result.
checks.b_evidence_source: Meaningful data charts showing survey results on pages 4, 5, 6, 8, and 10 (e.g., horizontal bar chart for sector representation on Page 4).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML rendering displays these charts as solid green rectangles with no data (e.g., appearing under the heading 'Q1: Which sector do you represent?').
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections Q1, Q2, Q3, Q5, and Q7

checks.a_analysis: No body content (paragraphs, full bulleted lists, or data) is visually duplicated in the HTML output. The appearance of headings in both the Table of Contents and the document body is an expected and intentional feature of accessible HTML design, not a content duplication defect.
